techniques.name: Bowline Knot
techniques.subtitle: The king of knots — a fixed loop that won't slip

steps.instruction: Form a small loop in the standing part of the rope.
steps.helpDetail: The 'rabbit hole.' The standing part is the long unused end. The working end is what you're tying with.

steps.instruction: Pass the working end UP through the loop.
steps.helpDetail: The rabbit comes out of the hole.

steps.instruction: Go around behind the standing part.
steps.helpDetail: The rabbit goes around the tree.

steps.instruction: Pass back DOWN through the same loop.
steps.helpDetail: The rabbit goes back in the hole. Pull tight. The bowline creates a fixed-size loop that will NOT tighten under load.

techniques.name: Clove Hitch
techniques.subtitle: Fast tie to a post or tree

steps.instruction: Wrap the rope around the post once.
steps.helpDetail: Start by going over the post, bringing the working end across the standing part.

steps.instruction: Cross over the first wrap and wrap around the post again.
steps.helpDetail: The second wrap should go OVER the standing part, creating an X pattern.

steps.instruction: Tuck the working end under the second wrap.
steps.helpDetail: Pull tight. The clove hitch grips under tension. Use for ridge poles, hanging clotheslines, and temporary anchors. Not for critical loads — it can slip.

techniques.name: Taut-Line Hitch
techniques.subtitle: Adjustable tension knot for guylines

steps.instruction: Loop rope around anchor (tree, stake).
steps.helpDetail: The working end comes back toward you with the rope going around the anchor point.

steps.instruction: Make 2 wraps inside the loop around the standing part.
steps.helpDetail: Coil the working end around the standing part INSIDE the loop — going toward the anchor. Two full wraps.

steps.instruction: Make 1 wrap outside the loop above the coils.
steps.helpDetail: One more wrap OUTSIDE (above) the loop around the standing part.

steps.instruction: Slide the knot to adjust tension.
steps.helpDetail: The taut-line hitch grips when loaded but slides when unloaded. Perfect for tent guylines, tarps, and clotheslines.

techniques.name: Square Lashing
techniques.subtitle: Join two poles at right angles

steps.instruction: Start with a clove hitch on the vertical pole.
steps.helpDetail: Tie immediately below where the horizontal pole will cross. This is your anchor.

steps.instruction: Weave over and under alternately around both poles.
steps.helpDetail: Go over the horizontal, under the vertical, over the horizontal, under the vertical. Work in a square pattern.

steps.instruction: Make 3-4 complete wrapping turns.
steps.helpDetail: Keep wraps tight and parallel. Each turn should be snug against the previous one.

steps.instruction: Frapping: wrap tightly between the poles to tighten.
steps.helpDetail: Frapping turns go between the poles (perpendicular to the lashing turns) and cinch everything tight. 2-3 frapping turns.

steps.instruction: Finish with a clove hitch on the horizontal pole.
steps.helpDetail: Square lashing is used for shelter frames, racks, bridges, ladders, and any structure requiring right-angle joints.

techniques.name: Field Knife Sharpening
techniques.subtitle: A dull knife is a dangerous knife

steps.instruction: Find a smooth, fine-grained stone (river rock works).
steps.helpDetail: Sandstone, granite, or any smooth stone. The bottom of a ceramic mug also works in civilized settings. Wet the stone.

steps.instruction: Hold blade at 15-20 degree angle to the stone.
steps.helpDetail: Lay blade flat, then raise spine about two nickels' thickness. Maintain this angle consistently — angle consistency matters more than angle precision.

steps.instruction: Sweep the blade across the stone in smooth strokes, alternating sides.
steps.helpDetail: Edge leading, sweeping from heel to tip. Maintain consistent pressure. 10 strokes per side, then alternate.

steps.instruction: Test sharpness: slice a leaf or shave arm hair.
steps.helpDetail: A sharp knife should slice paper or a leaf cleanly. If it tears, continue sharpening. Finish with lighter strokes for a fine edge.

techniques.name: Flint Knapping (Stone Tools)
techniques.subtitle: Make a cutting edge from stone

steps.instruction: Find flint, chert, obsidian, or quartz.
steps.helpDetail: These glassy, fine-grained stones fracture predictably. They're found near riverbeds, chalk deposits, and volcanic areas.

steps.instruction: Strike the edge with a harder hammerstone at a 45-degree angle.
steps.helpDetail: Strike downward and outward. The conchoidal fracture removes thin flakes. The flakes themselves are razor-sharp cutting tools.

steps.instruction: Use the sharp flakes as-is for cutting.
steps.helpDetail: Fresh flint flakes are sharper than surgical steel. Handle with care — wrap the non-cutting edge with bark or leather.

steps.instruction: Shape a larger piece into a hand axe by removing flakes around the edges.
steps.helpDetail: Work from both sides, thinning and shaping. This is the oldest human technology — 2.6 million years proven. It works.

techniques.name: Natural Fiber Cordage
techniques.subtitle: Make rope from plant fibers

steps.instruction: Gather long, strong plant fibers: inner bark, yucca, nettle, dogbane.
steps.helpDetail: Peel inner bark from dead trees. Soak tough fibers to soften. The longer the fibers, the stronger the cordage.

steps.instruction: Bundle 2-3 fibers and twist them tightly in one direction.
steps.helpDetail: Pinch the bundle at the midpoint. Twist AWAY from you. This is the 'Z-twist.'

steps.instruction: Fold the twisted bundle in half and let it spin back on itself.
steps.helpDetail: The two halves wrap around each other in the opposite direction (S-twist). This opposing twist is what creates strength.

steps.instruction: Add new fibers by overlapping 2-3 inches before the old run out.
steps.helpDetail: Stagger additions so splices don't align. This lets you make cordage of unlimited length. Test strength after each splice.

techniques.name: Improvised Containers
techniques.subtitle: Carry, store, and cook without pots

steps.instruction: Birch bark container: fold bark into box shape, pin with thorns.
steps.helpDetail: Birch bark peels easily and is waterproof. Score fold lines, fold up sides, pin corners with thorns or tie with cordage.

steps.instruction: Green bamboo section: cut between nodes for natural cup.
steps.helpDetail: The nodes form natural sealed bottoms. Cut above one node for a cup. Save longer sections for bottles.

steps.instruction: Stone bowl: find a concave rock and grind it deeper.
steps.helpDetail: Use a harder stone to abrade the cavity. River-smooth concave rocks are ideal starting points.

steps.instruction: Woven basket: weave flexible branches in over-under pattern.
steps.helpDetail: Willow, reed, or split bamboo. Start with a cross base, weave outward. Line with large leaves for waterproofing.

techniques.name: Making a Survival Spear
techniques.subtitle: Defense, hunting, and utility tool

steps.instruction: Find a straight hardwood pole 5-6 feet long.
steps.helpDetail: Green hardwood — ash, oak, hickory, or ironwood. Avoid softwoods (pine, willow) which dull quickly.

steps.instruction: Carve the tip with a knife or sharp stone into a point.
steps.helpDetail: Long, tapering point. A wider taper is stronger; a narrow taper is sharper. Compromise based on intended use.

steps.instruction: Fire-harden the tip by slowly rotating above hot coals.
steps.helpDetail: The heat case-hardens the wood. Rotate constantly to avoid charring. The tip should darken slightly and become extremely hard.

steps.instruction: For a better spear: lash a stone or metal blade to the tip.
steps.helpDetail: Split the shaft, insert the blade, and lash tightly with cordage. Apply pine resin over the lashing for waterproofing and adhesion.

techniques.name: Throwing Sling
techniques.subtitle: David's weapon — hunt and defend at range

steps.instruction: Cut two 24-inch lengths of cordage and a leather or woven pouch.
steps.helpDetail: The pouch holds the projectile. Cordage can be paracord, natural fiber, or even shoelaces. The pouch should cradle a fist-sized rock.

steps.instruction: Attach cords to each side of the pouch.
steps.helpDetail: One cord gets a finger loop (retention cord). The other gets a simple knot at the end (release cord).

steps.instruction: Load a round stone and swing overhead in a circular motion.
steps.helpDetail: Whirl 2-3 times overhead. Smooth, round stones fly straightest. Practice extensively before hunting.

steps.instruction: Release the knotted cord at the right moment to launch the projectile.
steps.helpDetail: Release when the stone is aimed at target (forward position in the circle). Effective range: 30-100 meters. Accurate with practice.

techniques.name: Field Gear Repair
techniques.subtitle: Fix equipment with natural materials

steps.instruction: Pine resin glue: melt pine sap and mix with charcoal dust.
steps.helpDetail: Equal parts pine resin and crushed charcoal + a small amount of fiber (rabbit droppings or dried grass). Heat until liquid, apply while warm.

steps.instruction: Patch holes in fabric with pine resin and a fabric patch.
steps.helpDetail: Apply warm resin to both surfaces. Press together. Let cool. This waterproof patch works on tents, packs, and clothing.

steps.instruction: Splint broken tool handles with sticks and cordage lashing.
steps.helpDetail: Place a shorter stick alongside the break. Lash tightly above and below the break. Apply pine resin over the lashing.

steps.instruction: Sew tears using a bone or thorn needle and plant fiber thread.
steps.helpDetail: Sharpen a thin bone fragment or use a strong thorn. Thread with inner bark fiber. Use a simple running stitch.

techniques.name: Camp Organization
techniques.subtitle: Efficient camp layout saves time and energy

steps.instruction: Fire pit at center, downwind from shelter.
steps.helpDetail: Wind should blow smoke AWAY from your sleeping area. Place fire close enough for warmth but far enough to prevent sparks igniting your shelter.

steps.instruction: Food storage 200 feet from camp, hung from a tree.
steps.helpDetail: This prevents wildlife encounters. Hang food at least 12 feet high and 6 feet from the trunk. Use a bear bag technique.

steps.instruction: Latrine 200 feet from camp, downhill from water source.
steps.helpDetail: Dig a cat hole 6-8 inches deep. Always downstream and downhill from your water source and camp. Cover after each use.

steps.instruction: Tool and supply area organized within arm's reach of shelter.
steps.helpDetail: Keep knife, fire kit, water, and first aid within reach even in the dark. Establish fixed locations so you find things by feel.

techniques.name: Carving a Fish Hook
techniques.subtitle: Bone, thorn, or wood—the oldest fishing technology

steps.instruction: Find a thorn, bone splinter, safety pin, or hardwood twig.
steps.helpDetail: Hawthorn thorns are natural hooks. Bone from a meal works.

steps.instruction: Gorge hook: sharpen both ends of a 1-inch splinter, tie in middle.
steps.helpDetail: Fish swallows it, it turns sideways and catches.

steps.instruction: Curved hook: heat and bend thorn/wire into J-shape with barb.
steps.helpDetail: Heat over fire to make bendable. Cut small notch for barb.

steps.instruction: Attach to line with clinch knot. Test pull before fishing.
steps.helpDetail: Thread, wrap 5 times, pull through loop.

techniques.name: Net Making
techniques.subtitle: Catch fish and small game with woven net

steps.instruction: Make cordage first—need 50-100 feet minimum.
steps.helpDetail: Natural fiber, paracord inner strands, or any thin strong string.

steps.instruction: Tie horizontal header line between two supports.
steps.helpDetail: This is top edge. The net hangs from this line.

steps.instruction: Tie vertical lines using sheet bend knots at intervals.
steps.helpDetail: 1-inch for minnows, 2-3 inches for larger fish.

steps.instruction: Weave horizontal rows, knotting at each intersection.
steps.helpDetail: Use mesh gauge stick for consistency. Sheet bend at each cross.

techniques.name: Bow & Arrow Making
techniques.subtitle: Hunting weapon from natural materials

steps.instruction: Find dead, dry hardwood stave — yew, ash, hickory, oak.
steps.helpDetail: 5-6 feet, 1.5 inches thick. No cracks. Dry and springy.

steps.instruction: Carve belly for flex, leave flat back intact.
steps.helpDetail: Never cut into the back. Taper from thick center to thin tips.

steps.instruction: Notch tips. String with cordage or sinew.
steps.helpDetail: String slightly shorter than bow. 6-8 inches of curve when strung.

steps.instruction: Arrows: straight dry sticks with sharpened tips and feather fletching.
steps.helpDetail: 2-3 feet, pencil thickness. Weight front more than back.

techniques.name: Stone Axe Construction
techniques.subtitle: Splitting and chopping with stone

steps.instruction: Find flat, hard stone with natural edge.
steps.helpDetail: River rocks often pre-shaped. Flat oval, fits in hand.

steps.instruction: Shape edge by pecking with harder stone.
steps.helpDetail: Strike along intended edge. Alternate sides. Wedge shape.

steps.instruction: Split green wood handle, wedge stone inside.
steps.helpDetail: 2-foot hardwood branch. Insert head. Bind with cordage.

steps.instruction: Wrap split with wet rawhide — shrinks as it dries.
steps.helpDetail: Creates incredibly strong bond. Figure-8 wrap pattern.

techniques.name: Bone Needle Making
techniques.subtitle: Sewing gear from animal bone

steps.instruction: Select a splinter of dense leg bone (deer/cow).
steps.helpDetail: Fresh bone relies on natural oils. Old bone is brittle. Shatter a leg bone to get splinters.

steps.instruction: Grind on sandstone to shape and sharpen.
steps.helpDetail: Rub against abrasive rock. Shape into a thick needle. Constant water lubrication helps.

steps.instruction: Drill the eye using a sharp flint flake or metal tip.
steps.helpDetail: Rotate the drill back and forth. Drill halfway from one side, then meet from the other to avoid splitting.

steps.instruction: Polish smooth to prevent fraying cordage.
steps.helpDetail: Smooth the eye hole carefully. Any rough edge will cut your thread.

techniques.name: Bark Container / Basket
techniques.subtitle: Carry water or berries with tree bark

steps.instruction: Cut a rectangle of flexible bark (birch, poplar, elm).
steps.helpDetail: Peel carefully in spring/summer when sap is running. Avoid damaging the tree if possible (use fallen log).

steps.instruction: Fold the corners inward to create a tub shape.
steps.helpDetail: Like folding a paper box. Determine the depth.

steps.instruction: Secure corners with split root or cordage stitches.
steps.helpDetail: Punch holes with an awl. Stitch the folds together.

steps.instruction: Seal seams with pine pitch for waterproofing.
steps.helpDetail: Melt pine resin mixed with charcoal. Smear over seams/holes to make it water-tight.

techniques.name: Advanced Bark Container
techniques.subtitle: Watertight folded container from tree bark

steps.instruction: Harvest a large rectangular sheet of fresh birch, elm, or poplar bark.
steps.helpDetail: Fresh bark is flexible and foldable. Dead bark cracks. Score the bark and peel carefully to avoid damaging the inner layer.

steps.instruction: Score fold lines for a box shape. Fold up the sides.
steps.helpDetail: Mark the base rectangle and score (don't cut through) the fold lines. Fold up all four sides. Overlap corners.

steps.instruction: Pin corners with wooden pegs or sew with root cordage.
steps.helpDetail: Spruce root makes excellent sewing material. Pre-drill holes with a bone awl, then lace the root through.

steps.instruction: Seal all seams with heated pine resin mixed with charcoal.
steps.helpDetail: Apply resin while warm along every seam. Let cool completely. Test by filling with water. Reseal any leaks.

techniques.name: Stone Drill (Pump Drill)
techniques.subtitle: Drill holes in wood, bone, and shell

steps.instruction: Knap a small, sharp stone point from flint or obsidian.
steps.helpDetail: The drill bit needs to be small (1/4 inch) and sharp. Pressure flaking gives the best control for small points.

steps.instruction: Lash the stone point to the end of a straight, round shaft.
steps.helpDetail: The shaft should be 12-18 inches long and perfectly straight. Split the tip, insert the stone, and wrap tightly with sinew.

steps.instruction: Attach a crossbar with string to create the pump mechanism.
steps.helpDetail: Drill a hole through the crossbar center, thread the shaft through. Attach strings from crossbar ends to the top of the shaft. Wind by spinning.

steps.instruction: Push down on the crossbar repeatedly. The string winds and unwinds, spinning the drill.
steps.helpDetail: Push down smoothly — the string unwinds, spinning the shaft. Let the crossbar rise — the string rewinds. Continuous pumping drills through wood and bone.

techniques.name: Fire-Hardened Spear
techniques.subtitle: Harden a wooden spear point with fire

steps.instruction: Select a straight hardwood sapling 5-7 feet long.
steps.helpDetail: Ash, oak, hickory, or yew are ideal. The shaft should be thumb-thick. Remove all branches and bark from the tip area.

steps.instruction: Carve the tip to a sharp point using a knife or sharp stone.
steps.helpDetail: Carve slowly, rotating the shaft. The point should be symmetrical and gradually tapered over 4-6 inches.

steps.instruction: Slowly rotate the tip over hot coals (NOT in flame).
steps.helpDetail: The goal is to drive out moisture and harden the wood cells without burning. Rotate constantly. The wood should darken to light brown, not blacken.

steps.instruction: The hardened point is now denser and more durable than raw wood.
steps.helpDetail: Fire-hardened points were used for millennia before stone tools. They can penetrate fish, small game, and serve as defensive weapons.

techniques.name: Sinew Cordage
techniques.subtitle: The strongest natural fiber from animal tendons

steps.instruction: Remove tendons from along the backbone or leg of a game animal.
steps.helpDetail: The long tendons running along the spine (backstrap sinew) are best. Leg tendons from deer, elk, or similar work well. Remove carefully in long strips.

steps.instruction: Dry the sinew completely — it becomes hard and brittle.
steps.helpDetail: Hang sinew strips in sun and wind. Fully dried sinew looks like thin white plastic. It stores indefinitely in this state.

steps.instruction: When needed, chew or soak the sinew to soften it.
steps.helpDetail: Chewing is traditional and effective — saliva softens sinew quickly. Once pliable, separate into thin fibers. Each fiber is incredibly strong.

steps.instruction: Twist or braid fibers into cord. Sinew shrinks as it dries, tightening bindings.
steps.helpDetail: This shrinking property makes sinew ideal for hafting (attaching stone to handles). Wrap wet sinew around the joint — as it dries, it contracts and locks tight.

techniques.name: Primitive Clay Pot
techniques.subtitle: Make a fireproof cooking vessel from river clay

steps.instruction: Collect clay from riverbanks or exposed soil cuts.
steps.helpDetail: Good clay is smooth, sticky when wet, and holds its shape. Test: roll a pencil-sized coil. If it doesn't crack when bent into a circle, it's usable clay.

steps.instruction: Mix clay with sand or crushed shell (temper) at a 4:1 ratio.
steps.helpDetail: Temper prevents cracking during drying and firing. Without temper, pure clay pots will crack. Crush shell or mix fine sand into the clay.

steps.instruction: Build the pot using coil method — roll ropes of clay and stack.
steps.helpDetail: Roll clay into finger-thick ropes. Coil in a circle for the base, then build walls by stacking coils. Smooth inside and outside surfaces as you go.

steps.instruction: Dry slowly for 1-2 days, then fire in a pit fire for 2-3 hours.
steps.helpDetail: Rapid drying or heating causes cracking. Dry in shade first. Place in fire gradually — start at the edge, move to center. After firing, the pot can withstand direct flame for cooking.

techniques.name: Atlatl (Spear Thrower)
techniques.subtitle: Triple your spear throwing distance and power

steps.instruction: Carve a flat board 18-24 inches long with a handle at one end.
steps.helpDetail: Hardwood works best. The board should be flat and straight. One end is your grip, the other end holds the spear.

steps.instruction: Carve or attach a small spur (hook) at the far end.
steps.helpDetail: The spur engages a dimple in the back of the dart/spear. This is the launch point — when you swing, the spur pushes the dart forward.

steps.instruction: Make lightweight darts 5-6 feet long with feather fletching.
steps.helpDetail: Atlatl darts are lighter and longer than hand-thrown spears. Straight, dried shafts with split-feather fletching for stable flight.

steps.instruction: Practice the overhand throwing motion — flick the wrist at the end.
steps.helpDetail: The atlatl effectively extends your arm by 2 feet, dramatically increasing dart velocity. Early humans used atlatls to hunt mammoths. Effective range: 30-40 yards.

techniques.name: Bone Fishhook
techniques.subtitle: Carve a functional fishhook from animal bone

steps.instruction: Select a thin, flat bone fragment from a rib or shoulder blade.
steps.helpDetail: Bird bones, fish bones, and small mammal ribs work well. The bone should be thin enough to carve but strong enough not to snap.

steps.instruction: Score the hook shape with a sharp stone — J-curve with a barb.
steps.helpDetail: Scratch the outline deeply on both sides. The hook should be 1-2 inches total. Include a small barb near the point to prevent fish from escaping.

steps.instruction: Snap away excess bone and refine the shape by grinding on stone.
steps.helpDetail: Carefully break along the score lines. Use a rough stone to grind the hook smooth and sharpen the point. The shank needs a notch for line attachment.

steps.instruction: Lash line to the shank using a snell knot or wrapping.
steps.helpDetail: Wrap the line around the shank several times and tie with a secure knot. Natural cordage, fishing line, or even unraveled paracord inner strands work.

techniques.name: Bark Cloth Processing
techniques.subtitle: Make flexible fabric from inner tree bark

steps.instruction: Harvest inner bark strips from paper mulberry, basswood, or elm.
steps.helpDetail: Peel in spring when bark separates easily. You want the inner bark (cambium layer), not the rough outer bark. Take from fallen or dead trees when possible.

steps.instruction: Soak the bark strips in water for 1-2 days.
steps.helpDetail: Soaking softens the fibers and makes them pliable. The bark should become flexible like wet leather when ready.

steps.instruction: Pound the soaked bark with a smooth stone or wooden mallet.
steps.helpDetail: Pound on a flat surface, rotating and folding. The fibers separate and interlock. Continue until the bark is thin, flexible, and fabric-like.

steps.instruction: Dry the bark cloth in the sun. It becomes a durable, flexible fabric.
steps.helpDetail: Bark cloth was used worldwide before woven textiles. It can be sewn, folded, and layered. Use for clothing, bandages, cordage backing, or shelter material.

techniques.name: Bamboo Multi-Tool Construction
techniques.subtitle: Create cutting, scraping, and stabbing tools from bamboo

steps.instruction: Select a green bamboo section at least 1 inch diameter.
steps.helpDetail: Green bamboo is flexible and won't splinter as badly as dry. Cut a section between two nodes for maximum strength. Bamboo edge cuts through skin easily — handle carefully even before shaping.

steps.instruction: Split lengthwise by scoring and applying pressure. Shape the edge with a rock.
steps.helpDetail: Score a line with a sharp rock, then apply pressure to split along the grain. The split edge is razor-sharp. For a knife: shape one end to a point. For a scraper: leave the edge wide. For a spear: fire-harden the tip.

steps.instruction: Fire-harden the edge by slowly rotating over hot coals. Do not burn.
steps.helpDetail: Hold the working edge 4-6 inches above coals. Rotate continuously. The heat drives out moisture and hardens the fibers. The edge should darken slightly but not char. Fire-hardened bamboo holds its edge remarkably well.

techniques.name: Natural Adhesive Making
techniques.subtitle: Create glue from pine resin, birch bark, or hide

steps.instruction: Collect pine or spruce resin from tree wounds. Look for amber-colored lumps.
steps.helpDetail: Conifers produce sticky resin at wound sites. Collect the hardened amber lumps — they peel off easily. You need about a golf ball-sized amount for useful quantities of glue.

steps.instruction: Melt resin over low heat. Mix with crushed charcoal (1:1 ratio) and optional plant fiber.
steps.helpDetail: Melt in a stone or shell vessel over coals. Pure resin is brittle when cool. Adding ground charcoal creates a flexible, strong adhesive. A pinch of plant fiber adds tensile strength. Stir thoroughly while hot.

steps.instruction: Apply hot to the joint. It sets in 1-2 minutes as it cools.
steps.helpDetail: Apply to both surfaces while molten. Press together firmly and hold for 2 minutes. Can be reheated and reapplied by warming near fire. This pitch glue is waterproof and strong enough for tool hafting and waterproofing containers.

techniques.name: Torch Construction
techniques.subtitle: Build a reliable portable light source

steps.instruction: Find a green hardwood stick 2-3 feet long and 1-2 inches thick.
steps.helpDetail: Green wood won't catch fire as easily as dry. Hardwood lasts longer than softwood. Split the top end into a cross pattern about 4 inches deep to create a cradle for fuel material.

steps.instruction: Pack the split top with birch bark, pine resin, cloth, or fatwood shavings.
steps.helpDetail: Birch bark burns bright and long. Pine resin-soaked materials burn brightest. Wrap tightly and secure with cordage. A torch wrapped with birch bark strips burns 20-30 minutes. Adding resin extends life.

steps.instruction: Light from existing fire. Hold at 45-degree angle to control dripping.
steps.helpDetail: Angle matters: too vertical drips hot material on your hand; too horizontal burns out faster. Walk slowly — wind will extinguish a fast-moving torch. Carry backup fuel material to repack when it burns low.

techniques.name: Improvised Fishing Hooks
techniques.subtitle: Create functional hooks from bone, wood, thorns, and wire

steps.instruction: Gorge hook: carve a 1-inch sliver of bone or hardwood pointed at both ends.
steps.helpDetail: The gorge hook is the oldest fishing technology. Sharpen both ends. Tie line to the middle. Bait covers the entire hook. When the fish swallows, pull — the gorge turns sideways and lodges in the throat.

steps.instruction: Thorn hook: select a strong thorn from hawthorn, honey locust, or similar tree.
steps.helpDetail: Natural thorns make excellent small hooks. Lash to a small twig at an angle to create the hook shape. Works best for small fish. Black locust and hawthorn thorns are ideal — strong, sharp, and curved.

steps.instruction: Composite hook: lash a sharp bone point to a hardwood shank at 45 degrees.
steps.helpDetail: Carve a shank from hardwood with a notch for the point. Lash a sharpened bone fragment at 45 degrees. Secure with pitch glue and cordage. This style is strong enough for large fish. Barb the point with a small notch.

techniques.name: Universal Trap Trigger Mechanisms
techniques.subtitle: Master the trigger systems used in all primitive traps

steps.instruction: Two-stick toggle trigger: carve two sticks with matching notches that hold under tension.
steps.helpDetail: Carve a hook shape on each stick that interlocks. One anchored to ground, one attached to the spring/weight. Tension holds them together. Slight movement releases. This is the basis for most snares and deadfalls.

steps.instruction: Figure-4 trigger: three sticks notched to create a free-standing support.
steps.helpDetail: Vertical post, horizontal bait stick, and diagonal support. Notches interlock to hold weight above. When bait stick is disturbed, the system collapses. Practice assembly 20+ times before field use — timing and fit are critical.

steps.instruction: Spring snare trigger: a peg-in-notch system attached to a bent sapling.
steps.helpDetail: Bend a sapling over the trail. Attach a trigger line to a toggle peg seated in a notched base stake. The noose loops around the peg. When an animal enters the noose and pulls, the peg releases and the sapling springs up.

techniques.name: Digging Stick & Pry Bar
techniques.subtitle: The most fundamental and versatile primitive tool

steps.instruction: Select a hardwood branch 3-4 feet long, 1.5-2 inches diameter.
steps.helpDetail: Hardwoods (oak, maple, hickory) are strongest. Green wood is better than dead wood for initial shaping. Select straight stock with no major knots or weak points.

steps.instruction: Sharpen one end to a chisel or point using a knife or sharp rock.
steps.helpDetail: For digging: chisel shape (flat edge) works best. For prying: wedge shape. For planting/probing: pointed tip. Shape the non-working end into a comfortable grip. Remove bark for better grip.

steps.instruction: Fire-harden the working end by slowly rotating over coals.
steps.helpDetail: This drives out moisture, compresses fibers, and creates a much harder surface. The tip should turn golden-brown, not black. A fire-hardened digging stick can break through hard clay and frozen ground.

techniques.name: Cobble Hammer & Hammerstone
techniques.subtitle: Create a basic impact tool for splitting, pounding, and shaping

steps.instruction: Find a dense, egg-shaped river cobble that fits comfortably in your hand.
steps.helpDetail: River rocks are pre-shaped by water and free of fracture lines. Granite, basalt, and quartzite are ideal. Avoid sandstone (too soft) and layered rocks (will shatter). Test by striking another rock — it should feel solid.

steps.instruction: For a hafted hammer: find a stone with a natural groove or pecked groove for binding.
steps.helpDetail: Wrap cordage in the groove and lash to a wooden handle. Green wood handle with a split end gripping the stone is strongest. Secure with wet rawhide — it shrinks as it dries, creating an incredibly tight bond.

steps.instruction: Use different faces for different tasks: flat for pounding, edge for splitting.
steps.helpDetail: The flat face crushes nuts and pounds stakes. An edge face splits wood. A pointed face chips other stones for tool making. One good hammerstone serves dozens of purposes — it's arguably the most versatile primitive tool.

techniques.name: Improvised Water Containers
techniques.subtitle: Build containers to collect, carry, and store water

steps.instruction: Birch bark container: fold bark into a box shape, secure with pins or cordage.
steps.helpDetail: Birch bark is waterproof and foldable when fresh. Score fold lines, fold up sides, and pin corners with wood pegs or sew with root cordage. Seal seams with pitch glue. Holds water for days.

steps.instruction: Animal stomach or bladder: turn inside out, rinse, and inflate to dry.
steps.helpDetail: After harvesting game, save the stomach and bladder. Rinse thoroughly, turn inside out, and inflate. Once dry, they make excellent water carriers. The stomach can hold 1-2 liters. Tie the openings with cordage.

steps.instruction: Bamboo water bottle: cut a section with one node intact as the bottom.
steps.helpDetail: Cut bamboo at an angle just above a node (sealed end = bottom). The natural tube holds water perfectly. Carve a wooden plug for the top. A 4-inch diameter bamboo section holds about 1 liter. Carry multiple.

techniques.name: Improvised Snow Goggles
techniques.subtitle: Prevent snow blindness with improvised eye protection

steps.instruction: Select material: birch bark, cardboard, leather, or any opaque flexible sheet.
steps.helpDetail: Snow blindness (UV keratitis) causes temporary but extremely painful blindness within hours of bright snow exposure. Even overcast days in snow reflect enough UV to cause damage. Prevention is critical — treatment is rest in darkness.

steps.instruction: Cut a strip to cover both eyes. Cut narrow horizontal slits (1mm) at each eye position.
steps.helpDetail: The slits should be about 1-2 inches wide and as narrow as possible while still allowing vision. Narrower = better UV protection. You need enough vision to navigate but minimal light entry.

steps.instruction: Attach cordage or elastic to wrap around the head. Blacken the inner surface.
steps.helpDetail: Darken the inside surface with charcoal or soot to reduce reflective glare. Secure with cordage tied behind the head. These goggles reduce UV exposure by 90%+ and are the difference between navigating and incapacitation.

techniques.name: Hand Drill Fire Optimization
techniques.subtitle: Improve your hand drill technique for reliable fire

steps.instruction: Select spindle: dead, dry, straight, pithy stem. Mullein, cattail, or elderberry ideal.
steps.helpDetail: The spindle must be straight, 18-24 inches long, and 3/8 to 1/2 inch diameter. Pithy stems work best — the soft center creates more friction. Dead and dry is critical. Green wood will never produce enough heat.

steps.instruction: Floating hand technique: alternate hands downward while maintaining speed and pressure.
steps.helpDetail: The biggest hand drill failure is losing speed while resetting hand position. Float your hands down the spindle with continuous downward pressure and rotation. When you reach the bottom, quickly snap back to the top without stopping.

steps.instruction: Add friction enhancers: sand in the notch, and moisture from crushed green plants on your palms.
steps.helpDetail: A pinch of fine sand in the hearth board notch increases friction dramatically. Rub crushed green plantain leaves on your palms — the slight tackiness prevents slipping. These two additions make the hand drill significantly easier.

techniques.name: Figure-4 Deadfall Trap
techniques.subtitle: Build a crushing trap for small game

steps.instruction: Carve three sticks: vertical post, horizontal bait bar, diagonal support.
steps.helpDetail: Each stick needs specific notches to interlock. Vertical: flat notch at top, square notch at bottom. Diagonal: point at bottom, flat notch at top matching vertical. Horizontal: square notch to engage vertical, bait on far end.

steps.instruction: Assemble the three sticks into a figure-4 shape under a heavy flat rock.
steps.helpDetail: The rock must be heavy enough to kill instantly — 5x the animal's weight minimum. A 2-pound squirrel needs a 10-pound rock. Test the trigger by gently touching the bait bar — it should collapse with light touch.

steps.instruction: Bait with local food sources. Place along animal trails with guide fences.
steps.helpDetail: Build small stick fences on either side to funnel the animal toward the bait. Smear bait on the horizontal stick. Place at trail junctions or near den entrances. Set at dusk, check at dawn. Reset daily.

techniques.name: Basic Hide Tanning
techniques.subtitle: Preserve animal hides for clothing, cordage, and containers

steps.instruction: Flesh the hide: scrape all meat, fat, and membrane from the inner surface.
steps.helpDetail: Stretch the hide on a frame or flat log. Use a scraper made from bone, rock edge, or split hardwood. Remove every trace of flesh — any remaining will rot and stink. This is the most critical and time-consuming step.

steps.instruction: Brain tan: mash the animal's brain and rub into the hide thoroughly.
steps.helpDetail: Every animal has enough brain to tan its own hide. Mash brain with warm water into a paste. Rub into every square inch of the hide, both sides. The oils in the brain preserve and soften the leather. Let soak overnight.

steps.instruction: Smoke the hide over a slow smoldering fire to set the tan.
steps.helpDetail: After brain tanning and drying, the leather will stiffen if it gets wet — unless smoked. Drape over a smoldering fire (punk wood, no flame) for 2-4 hours per side. The smoke locks in the oils and makes the hide permanently soft.

techniques.name: Charcoal Water Filter Construction
techniques.subtitle: Build a gravity-fed filter from natural materials

steps.instruction: Make charcoal: burn hardwood to coals, then smother. Crush into small pieces.
steps.helpDetail: Good charcoal is jet black, lightweight, and breaks clean. Burns to coals then cover with dirt or ash to smother. Once cool, crush to pea-sized or smaller. Charcoal absorbs chemicals, bacteria, and improves taste.

steps.instruction: Layer in container: coarse gravel (bottom), fine sand, crushed charcoal, fine sand, grass (top).
steps.helpDetail: Use a hollow log, birch bark tube, or plastic bottle with bottom cut off. Layers from bottom: rocks → gravel → sand → charcoal → sand → grass. Each layer ~2 inches. The grass pre-filters debris, charcoal does the heavy work.

steps.instruction: Pour water through top. Collect filtered water below. STILL BOIL before drinking.
steps.helpDetail: This filter removes sediment, improves taste, and reduces (not eliminates) bacteria. The charcoal absorbs many chemical contaminants. But it does NOT reliably kill all pathogens — always boil filtered water before drinking.

techniques.name: Improvised Cutting Tools
techniques.subtitle: Create saws and cutting edges from found materials

steps.instruction: Wire saw: twist two strands of wire together. Loop handles at each end.
steps.helpDetail: Salvaged wire from fencing, vehicles, or electronics works. Twist two strands tight for rigidity. Loops at each end fit over sticks for handles. Draw back and forth — cuts wood surprisingly fast. Works on bone too.

steps.instruction: Stone edge tool: find flint, obsidian, or fine-grained stone and strike flakes.
steps.helpDetail: Hard percussion (striking with a hammerstone) removes large flakes. Soft percussion (antler or hardwood baton) removes thinner, sharper flakes. A fresh flake edge is sharper than surgical steel. Handle carefully.

steps.instruction: Tin can knife: cut a can in half, fold the edge over for a handle.
steps.helpDetail: An aluminum can edge is razor-sharp when cut. Fold one edge over several times for a grip. The working edge cuts cordage, flesh, and thin materials. Wrap the handle in cloth or bark to prevent cuts. Short lifespan but immediate results.

techniques.name: Improvised Fishing & Trapping Net
techniques.subtitle: Construct nets from natural cordage for fishing and trapping

steps.instruction: Make cordage from plant fibers: dogbane, nettle, milkweed, or inner bark.
steps.helpDetail: Split stems lengthwise, scrape out pith, and twist the outer fibers into cordage using the reverse-twist method. You need a LOT of cordage — a small net requires 50+ feet. Start cordage production as a background task every day.

steps.instruction: Tie a mesh net using sheet bend knots or overhand knots at regular intervals.
steps.helpDetail: Use a gauge stick to keep mesh openings consistent. Smaller mesh = smaller fish caught. 1-inch mesh for pan fish, 2-inch for larger. Work row by row, tying each new cord to the previous row's loops.

steps.instruction: Attach weights (stones) to the bottom edge and floats (bark pieces) to the top.
steps.helpDetail: The net must hang vertically in water. Stones every 6 inches on the bottom, bark or wood floats on top. Set across a stream with stake anchors. Fish swim into the mesh and become tangled. Check every few hours.

articles.title: Essential Knots Every Survivor Must Know
articles.body:
You don't need to know 50 knots. You need to know 5 perfectly.

The essential five:
1. Bowline — fixed loop, rescue, tying to rings/anchors
2. Clove Hitch — quick tie to posts, ridge poles, temporary anchors
3. Taut-Line Hitch — adjustable tension for guylines and shelters
4. Sheet Bend — joining two ropes of different diameters
5. Figure-8 — stopper knot, climbing anchor

Knot principles:
- A knot reduces rope strength by 20-50%. The sharper the bends, the more strength lost.
- Wet knots are harder to untie — use knots with easy release in wet conditions.
- All knots should be dressed (tightened neatly) and set (loaded gently before heavy use).
- Practice until you can tie them in the dark, wet, with cold hands.

Lashing vs knots:
Lashings join structural members (shelter building). Knots connect ropes or ropes to objects. Learn both. Square lashing for right angles, diagonal lashing for X-joints, sheer lashing for A-frames.

articles.title: The Survival Knife: Your Most Important Tool
articles.body:
If you can carry only one tool into the wilderness, carry a knife. It makes every other tool.

With a knife you can:
- Build shelter (cut branches, split wood, carve stakes)
- Start fire (baton wood, make feather sticks, throw sparks with ferro rod)
- Get food (carve snares, spear, fishhooks, process game)
- Make tools (carve bowls, spoons, handles, digging sticks)
- First aid (cut bandages, remove splinters, improvise tools)

Ideal survival knife:
- Full-tang fixed blade (folding knives break under stress)
- 4-5 inch blade (large enough to baton, small enough for detail)
- High carbon steel (easier to sharpen in the field than stainless)
- Flat or scandi grind (most versatile for bushcraft)
- No serrations (hard to sharpen without tools)

Field care:
- Clean and dry after each use
- Oil the blade with any fat to prevent rust
- Sharpen regularly — 10 strokes per side daily
- Never throw your knife (you risk losing or breaking it)
- Always cut away from your body

Batoning: split wood by placing the knife edge on the end of a log and striking the spine with another piece of wood. This splits wood without an axe.

articles.title: Field Repair & Improvisation
articles.body:
In survival, everything breaks eventually. The ability to repair and improvise separates survivors from victims.

Universal repair materials found in nature:
- Pine resin: waterproof glue, sealant, fire starter, torch fuel
- Plant cordage: rope, thread, lashing, fishing line
- Bone: needles, hooks, toggles, awls, scrapers
- Rock: hammers, anvils, scrapers, cutting edges
- Clay: pottery, ovens, sealant, fire reflectors
- Birch bark: containers, shingles, fire starter, waterproofing

Pine resin recipe (the survival super-glue):
1. Collect hardened sap from pine/spruce trees
2. Heat slowly until liquid (do not boil)
3. Mix 1:1 with crushed charcoal
4. Add small amount of plant fiber
5. Apply while warm — hardens as it cools

Mental framework: Every object has multiple uses. A belt is a strap, a tourniquet, a strop, a lashing. A tarp is a shelter, a rain collector, a carrying bag, a signaling device. Think in terms of material properties (strong, sharp, flexible, waterproof) not intended use.

articles.title: Camp Setup & Management
articles.body:
A well-organized camp is a force multiplier. Disorganization wastes time, energy, and morale.

Camp selection criteria:
- Water: within 200 yards but not in a flood plain
- Shelter: protected from wind, not under dead trees/branches
- Fire: dry ground, fuel nearby, fire reflector potential
- Signals: visible from air/ground, near open area
- Hazards: away from animal trails, ant hills, hornet nests, cliff edges

Camp zones (concentric rings):
1. Sleep zone: warmest, most protected, clean
2. Work zone: fire, cooking, tool maintenance
3. Storage zone: food cache, water storage, firewood
4. Waste zone: latrine, garbage pit, fish cleaning

Firewood rule of three:
- The firewood you think you need × 3 = what you actually need
- Gather firewood before dark — ALWAYS
- Sort by size: tinder, kindling, fuel, overnight logs

Defensive camp features:
- Noise-makers on perimeter approach lines (cans with rocks, sticks)
- Fire maintained through night (deters most wildlife)
- Food hung 12+ feet, 200+ feet from camp
- Clear sightlines around shelter

articles.title: Natural Cordage: The Universal Survival Material
articles.body:
Cord connects everything in survival — shelters, traps, fishing, weapons, and tools. Natural cordage is everywhere if you know where to look.

Best natural cordage materials:
- Plant fibers: dogbane, milkweed, stinging nettle, yucca, cattail leaves
- Bark fibers: basswood inner bark, cedar bark, willow bark
- Animal: sinew (strongest), rawhide strips, gut
- Roots: spruce roots, pine roots (flexible and strong)

Basic twist cordage technique:
1. Gather long fibers and bundle them
2. Divide into two equal bundles
3. Twist each bundle clockwise individually
4. Wrap the two bundles counter-clockwise around each other
5. The opposing twists lock each other in place

Splicing (adding length):
- When a bundle runs thin, insert new fibers alongside the short ones
- Stagger splices — never splice both bundles at the same point
- A well-made cordage splice is nearly as strong as unspliced cord

Strength comparison:
- Paracord inner strand: 50 lbs
- Good plant cordage (2-ply): 30-50 lbs
- Sinew cord: 80+ lbs
- Rawhide strip: 100+ lbs

articles.title: The Stone Age Toolkit
articles.body:
For 2.5 million years, stone was humanity's primary material. The skills still work.

Essential stone tools:
1. Chopper: crude cutting edge on a river cobble — the simplest stone tool
2. Hand axe: bifacially flaked teardrop shape — cuts, scrapes, digs
3. Scraper: steep-edged flake for hide processing and wood shaping
4. Knife: thin, long flake with sharp edge — the most useful daily tool
5. Drill point: small, triangular piece for piercing bone, shell, and wood

Best rocks for tools:
- Flint/chert: king of toolmaking, conchoidal fracture, razor edges
- Obsidian: volcanic glass, sharpest edges in nature (thinner than steel scalpels)
- Quartzite: tough, holds up for chopping and hammering
- Jasper: fine-grained, makes excellent small tools
- Basalt: coarse but good for heavy-duty choppers and hammers

Collecting strategy:
- Riverbeds are natural rock shops — water sorts by density and hardness
- Look for rocks that ring when tapped (indicates fine grain)
- Avoid rocks with visible cracks, inclusions, or layering
- Carry good stone when you find it — the perfect rock may not be at your camp

articles.title: Improvised Containers
articles.body:
Containers solve countless problems: carry water, cook food, store supplies, collect rain, process materials.

Natural containers:
- Bamboo segments: naturally waterproof between nodes. Cut above and below a node for instant cup.
- Gourds: dried gourds are lightweight, durable, waterproof
- Coconut shells: two bowls from one coconut
- Large leaves: folded and pinned with thorns for temporary liquid transport
- Hollow logs: natural troughs for water storage

Constructed containers:
- Birch bark: folded and sealed with resin (waterproof, can boil water)
- Clay pots: fired clay holds liquid, cooks over fire, stores food
- Woven baskets: coiled or woven, lined with clay for water-tightness
- Animal stomach/bladder: natural waterproof bag (used for millennia)
- Hide: shaped when wet, dries into rigid container (parfleche)

Hot rock cooking (no fireproof container needed):
1. Dig a small pit
2. Line with hide, bark, or large leaves
3. Fill with water
4. Heat rocks in fire until glowing
5. Drop rocks into water with sticks
6. Water boils in minutes
7. Add food to cook

This technique works with any container that holds water, even if it can't go on fire.

articles.title: Natural Adhesives: The Complete Guide
articles.body:
Adhesives were among the earliest technologies humans developed. Understanding them transforms your tool-making capability.

Pine pitch glue (strongest natural adhesive):
- Collect hardened resin from conifer wounds
- Melt over low heat (resin is flammable — keep away from flame)
- Mix 1:1 with crushed charcoal for flexibility
- Optional: add plant fiber for tensile strength
- Apply hot, sets in 1-2 minutes
- Waterproof and strong enough for tool hafting
- Can be reheated and reused indefinitely

Birch bark tar (superior but harder to make):
- Requires sealed heating of birch bark (pyrolysis)
- Pack bark tightly in a closed container with a small exit hole
- Heat from outside — tar drips out the hole
- Produces a black, waterproof, extremely strong adhesive
- Used for tens of thousands of years across Northern Europe

Hide glue:
- Boil rawhide scraps or sinew until dissolved
- The collagen creates a strong, clear adhesive
- Sets rigid when cool, can be reheated
- Excellent for fletching arrows and laminating wood

All natural adhesives are thermoplastic — they soften with heat. Store pitch glue sticks on a cord for easy transport and reuse.

articles.title: Primitive Fishing Technology
articles.body:
Fish are among the easiest wild protein sources to harvest. Passive methods catch food while you do other survival work.

Passive fishing methods (set and forget):
- Trotline: long line strung across water with multiple baited hooks at intervals
- Fish trap: woven funnel-entry basket in a stream (fish enter, can't exit)
- Gill net: mesh net stretched across current (fish swim in, gills tangle)
- Bank lines: baited hooks tied to flexible branches along shore

Active fishing methods:
- Spearing: requires clear, shallow water and practice
- Hand fishing (noodling): reach into underwater cavities for catfish
- Tickling: gently rub a fish's belly from below, then grab
- Improvised rod and line: any pole, cordage, and hook

Bait hierarchy (most effective to least):
1. Live insects (grasshoppers, grubs, worms)
2. Cut bait (piece of another fish)
3. Bright colored material (cloth, feather lures)
4. Meat scraps
5. Berries and bread-like dough

Location matters more than bait:
- Fish rest behind rocks in current (eddies)
- Under fallen trees and overhanging banks
- At the confluence of streams
- In the deepest pool of a stream section
- Near underwater structure (roots, rocks, logs)

Caloric investment rule: passive methods yield more calories per effort than active methods. Set 5-10 lines/traps and focus your time elsewhere.

articles.title: Principles of Effective Trapping
articles.body:
Trapping is the most calorie-efficient way to acquire protein in a survival situation.

Why trapping beats hunting:
- Traps work 24/7 while you sleep, build shelter, and signal
- No stalking skill or stealth required
- One person can run 10-20 traps per day
- Smaller animals are more abundant and easier to trap
- No weapons required

Trap placement rules:
1. Set on active trails (look for tracks, droppings, fur)
2. Use guide sticks to funnel animals into the trap
3. Place where terrain naturally constricts movement
4. Set near water sources (all animals drink)
5. Set near food sources (fruiting trees, berry patches)

The numbers game:
- Expect a 10-20% success rate per trap per night
- Set 10+ traps to reliably catch 1-2 animals per day
- Vary trap types (snares, deadfalls, pit traps)
- Check and reset every 12 hours

Common trap types by target:
- Squirrels: pole snare (leaning pole with multiple snare loops)
- Rabbits: trail snare (noose at head height on a trail)
- Birds: ojibwa bird pole (perch snare)
- Rats/mice: figure-4 deadfall
- Fish: funnel trap, trotline

Ethical and legal caveat: trapping is regulated everywhere. In true survival, use every method available. In practice, learn the laws of your area.

articles.title: Hide & Leather Working in Survival
articles.body:
Animal hides are among the most valuable survival resources. A single deer hide provides material for clothing, cordage, containers, and shelter.

What you can make from one hide:
- Moccasins (foot protection — critical)
- Gloves or mittens
- Cordage (rawhide strips are incredibly strong)
- Water container or carrying bag
- Drum head (for signaling)
- Patches for clothing repair
- Sling or carry strap

Processing methods:

Rawhide (no tanning — just dried):
- Clean, flesh, stretch, and dry
- Rigid when dry, flexible when wet
- Use for cordage, lashing, snowshoe webbing
- Shrinks 10-15% as it dries (use this for tight bindings)

Brain tan (soft leather):
- Flesh → brain → stretch → smoke
- Produces soft, flexible, breathable leather
- Washable (the smoking step makes it water-resistant)
- Best for clothing and moccasins

Smoke tan (simplified):
- Flesh → stretch → smoke directly
- Less soft than brain tan but faster
- Adequate for bags, patches, and lashing

Time investment: processing one deer hide takes 8-16 hours of active work spread over 2-4 days. The result is a material more versatile than any modern equivalent in a primitive context.

articles.title: Stone Tool Making: Flintknapping Basics
articles.body:
Stone tools sustained humanity for 2.5 million years. The skills are still relevant in survival.

Best stones for tools (conchoidal fracture required):
- Flint/chert: most common, found in limestone regions
- Obsidian: volcanic glass, sharpest edges possible
- Quartzite: common, harder to work but available everywhere
- Jasper: colorful, works like flint
- Agate: similar to jasper

Basic knapping technique:
1. Select a core stone with no visible cracks
2. Strike with a hammerstone at 45-degree angle on the edge
3. Flakes pop off the opposite side (they travel through the stone)
4. Each flake is a potential tool — razor-sharp edge
5. For finer work, use an antler or hardwood baton

Safety rules:
- Flake edges are sharper than surgical steel
- Wear eye protection if possible (stone chips fly)
- Work on your lap with a leather pad
- Never touch the edges with bare fingers
- Store tools in protective wrapping

Tool types from flakes:
- Scraper: thick flake used for hide processing
- Blade: long, thin flake for cutting
- Point: shaped by pressure flaking for arrows and spears
- Drill: narrow point for making holes in wood, bone, and leather
- Chopper: large core tool for heavy cutting

A skilled knapper can produce a usable blade in under a minute. Practice with river cobbles before attempting fine-grained stones.
